name: Viracocha
description: Viracocha, also known as Wiraqucha, is a prominent creator deity in the pre-Inca and Inca mythology of the Andean region. He is considered one of the most important deities in the Inca pantheon, often depicted as a bearded man, a somewhat unusual representation in pre-Columbian art. Viracocha is attributed with creating the universe, the sun, the moon, the stars, and humanity. He is also associated with civilization, order, and the introduction of agriculture and other essential skills. The mythology surrounding Viracocha is complex and varied, with different versions of his creation stories and his role in the world's development. Some accounts portray him as a wandering god who traveled throughout the Andes, teaching people and establishing order before eventually disappearing across the ocean. His worship was particularly important to the Inca emperors, who often claimed descent from him to legitimize their rule, emphasizing his role as a civilizing hero and a source of divine authority. The precise nature of his worship and beliefs surrounding him are still debated among scholars, but his significance in Andean cosmology is undeniable.
subtitle: The Creator God

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the World
keyMyths.description: Viracocha is said to have emerged from Lake Titicaca, a sacred body of water in the Andes, and created the world. Initially, he created a world without sun, moon, or stars, populated by mindless giants. Displeased with his first attempt, he destroyed the giants with a great flood and then created a new world with the sun, moon, stars, and humans. This myth highlights Viracocha's power as a creator and his role in establishing the current order of the cosmos.
keyMyths.source: Bauer, Brian S. *The Sacred Landscape of the Inca: The Cusco Ceque System*. University of Texas Press, 1998.

keyMyths.title: The Flood and Re-Creation
keyMyths.description: The myth of the flood is a recurring theme in Andean mythology, often attributed to Viracocha's dissatisfaction with humanity. After creating the initial world, he sent a devastating flood to destroy the unruly giants and cleanse the earth. Following the flood, Viracocha re-created humanity from small stones or clay, imbuing them with intelligence and the ability to cultivate the land, marking a new beginning for the world.
keyMyths.source: Classen, Constance. *Worlds of Sense: Exploring the Senses in History and Across Cultures*. Routledge, 1993.

keyMyths.title: Viracocha's Journey
keyMyths.description: After creating the world and humanity, Viracocha embarked on a journey, traveling throughout the Andes as a wandering teacher and civilizer. He is said to have taught people agriculture, weaving, and other essential skills. He also established laws and social order before eventually disappearing across the ocean, promising to return one day. This journey emphasizes his role as a benevolent and wise figure.
keyMyths.source: Hyslop, John. *The Inka Road System*. Academic Press, 1984.

sources.author: Bauer, Brian S.
sources.work: The Sacred Landscape of the Inca: The Cusco Ceque System
sources.description: Examines the Inca's cosmological and social organization, including the significance of Viracocha within their sacred landscape.
sources.text: Examines the Inca's cosmological and social organization, including the significance of Viracocha within their sacred landscape.

sources.author: Classen, Constance
sources.work: Worlds of Sense: Exploring the Senses in History and Across Cultures
sources.description: Explores how different cultures, including the Inca, perceive and interact with the world through their senses and how this relates to their mythology.

sources.author: Hyslop, John
sources.description: Provides detailed information on the Inca road system, which facilitated the spread of cultural and religious ideas, including the worship of Viracocha.
